foundation.title: STRUCTURE
foundation.subtitle: Basic Program
foundation.content: A Python program focuses on clean execution without boilerplate. It executes top-to-bottom, line-by-line. Statements are simple lines of code, and sequence is critical.
foundation.scenario: A Grocery List. You start at the top, buy the first item, then move down. You don't have to announce 'Start Shopping', you just start.
foundation.learningOutcome: Code runs line by line in order

examples.title: Top-Down Run
examples.explanation: The CPU reads line 1, finishes it, then moves to line 2.

examples.title: Independent Line
examples.explanation: Every line can be a complete thought.

examples.title: Entry Point
examples.explanation: Python starts right at line 1. No main() needed.

foundation.title: PRINT
foundation.subtitle: Display Output
foundation.content: The print() function is your program's voice. It takes text, numbers, or logic and blasts it to the console. You can print numbers directly, but text needs quotes.
foundation.scenario: A Megaphone. Whatever you whisper into the microphone gets blasted out to the whole world.

examples.title: Printing Text
examples.explanation: Printing standard text requires safe quotes.

examples.title: Printing Numbers
examples.explanation: Values don't need quotes and can do math.

examples.title: Multiple
examples.explanation: Using multiple statements in a row.

foundation.keyPoint: print() outputs data to the screen.

foundation.title: FORMAT
foundation.subtitle: Visual Output
foundation.content: Formatting allows you to combine text and values beautifully. You can control spaces, new lines (\n), and seamlessly inject values into strings using f-strings.
foundation.scenario: A Fill-in-the-Blank Letter. 'Dear {name}', the curly braces are empty slots where you insert real data.

examples.title: The f-string
examples.explanation: Placing values directly inside {} within a string.

examples.title: New Line
examples.explanation: Using \n to break lines manually.

examples.title: Multiple Values
examples.explanation: Separating items with commas intelligently adds spaces.

foundation.keyPoint: Format output quickly using f-strings or commas.

foundation.title: VARIABLE
foundation.subtitle: Data Containers
foundation.content: Variables are named boxes that hold data in memory. You need them because data changes (like a player's score). They let you store, change, and track information.
foundation.scenario: A Moving Box. You write 'Kitchen Stuff' (Name) and put plates strictly inside (Value). Default data containers for the engine.

examples.title: Declaration
examples.explanation: Giving a box a name and placing data inside.

examples.title: Usage
examples.explanation: Using the box's name to use the data.

examples.title: Why Needed
examples.explanation: Variables make logic readable and dynamic.

foundation.keyPoint: Variables remember changing things in memory.

foundation.title: NAME_RULES
foundation.subtitle: Naming Rules
foundation.content: Variables have strict naming rules. Meaningful naming is a best practice. They must use allowed characters, with absolutely no spaces and cannot start with a number.
foundation.scenario: A System Password. You must use standard text characters, no weird spaces, or the parser rejects it completely.
foundation.learningOutcome: Write valid and readable variable names

examples.title: Meaningful Name
examples.explanation: Perfect names are clear and use underscores.

examples.title: Invalid Start
examples.explanation: A number at the start crashes Python.

examples.title: No Spaces
examples.explanation: Spaces break the text apart.

foundation.keyPoint: Start with a letter/_. No spaces allowed.

foundation.title: DATATYPES
foundation.subtitle: The 4 Core Types
foundation.content: Data is divided into Types. Python relies on Integer (whole positive/negative), Float (decimals), String (text in quotes), and Boolean (True/False logic).
foundation.scenario: A Recycling Bin. Glass goes in the Glass bin (String). Paper goes in Paper (Int). If you mix them blindly, the recycling machine breaks.
foundation.learningOutcome: Identify and use correct data types

examples.title: Whole Ints
examples.explanation: Positive or negative numbers with no fraction.

examples.title: Floats & Bools
examples.explanation: Decimals and logic flags.

examples.title: Strings
examples.explanation: Text enclosed safely entirely in quotes.

foundation.title: ASSIGN
foundation.subtitle: Storing Data
foundation.content: Assignment is the act of putting data into a variable box using the '=' operator. You can safely store numbers, strings, and booleans sequentially.
foundation.scenario: A Shipping Label. You calculate the package's weight first (Right Side), and ONLY THEN do you write it on the label (Left Side).

examples.title: Assign Text
examples.explanation: Storing a fresh string to a label.

examples.title: Assign Math
examples.explanation: Storing complex math cleanly to a label.

examples.title: Assign Logic
examples.explanation: Storing powerful true/false logic directly.

foundation.title: REASSIGN
foundation.subtitle: Dynamic Values
foundation.content: Variables are dynamic. You can change their value entirely, overwriting the old data. The 'latest' value is all the computer strictly remembers.
foundation.scenario: A Lockbox. You take out the golden coin, throw it away, and put in a silver coin. The box is the same, but the inner content completely vanished and was replaced.

examples.title: The Overwrite
examples.explanation: Changing a variable's memory content completely.

examples.title: Step-by-Step
examples.explanation: Variables update as the program goes down.

examples.title: Self-Update
examples.explanation: Using the old value to brutally calculate the new value.

foundation.keyPoint: Variables can brutally overwrite old values at any time.

foundation.title: INPUT_CONCEPT
foundation.subtitle: Interaction flow
foundation.content: Input allows user interaction. The system waits, takes user input conceptually, stores it, and then uses it. It is the direct opposite of Output.
foundation.scenario: A Vending Machine. You press a button (Input), it processes, and gives a snack (Output). A continuous conceptual cycle.

examples.title: Wait for User
examples.explanation: Pausing the entire script until action occurs.

examples.title: Store & Use
examples.explanation: Capturing the data to use instantly below.

examples.title: Contrast
examples.explanation: Input goes IN to memory, Output goes OUT to eyes.

foundation.keyPoint: Input brings data IN. Output pushes data OUT.

foundation.title: COMMENTS
foundation.subtitle: Ignored text
foundation.content: Comments are notes for humans to keep code readable. The computer entirely ignores them. Single-line comments start perfectly with a hashtag (#).
foundation.scenario: A Sticky Note on an Engine. The note says 'Do not touch the red wire!'. The engine doesn't read the note, but the mechanic does safely.

examples.title: Single-Line
examples.explanation: Taking up a whole line to explain logic.

examples.title: Ignored Logic
examples.explanation: Testing lines by hiding them from Python.

examples.title: Readability
examples.explanation: Explaining the WHY, not the What.

foundation.keyPoint: # makes Python ignore the rest of the line.

foundation.title: KEYWORDS
foundation.subtitle: Reserved syntax
foundation.content: Keywords are completely reserved system words that Python uses for its own internal structure. You absolutely cannot use them as variable names to avoid mistakes.
foundation.scenario: Reserved Parking. The spot marked 'CEO' (Keyword) cannot be casually parked in by 'Employee' (Variable). The system aggressively rejects you.

examples.title: System Logic
examples.explanation: Internal reserved words forming flow.

examples.title: The Error
examples.explanation: Naming a variable 'if' shatters the language parser.

examples.title: Syntax Rules
examples.explanation: Recognizing reserved words prevents major bugs.

foundation.keyPoint: Keyword = Reserved system word you cannot hijack.

foundation.title: CASE
foundation.subtitle: Case Sensitivity
foundation.content: Uppercase and lowercase are vastly different in programming. Case mismatch causes instant logical errors because 'Apple' and 'apple' are two separate objects.
foundation.scenario: Two Cloned Twins. They look completely identical, but one is named 'John' and the other 'john'. In Python, they are two heavily disconnected people.
foundation.learningOutcome: Avoid logical errors due to case mismatch

examples.title: Separate Boxes
examples.explanation: Changing case strictly makes a totally fresh container.

examples.title: Strict Language
examples.explanation: Python system commands are always lower case.

examples.title: Case Crash
examples.explanation: Forgetting the exact case you typed will cause "Not Defined" crashes.

foundation.title: PREDICT
foundation.subtitle: Output Tracing
foundation.content: Output tracing means reading code line-by-line, perfectly predicting variable changes manually like a CPU. It prevents common beginner execution mistakes.
foundation.scenario: Playing 3D Chess purely in your Head. You picture entirely exactly what the board (RAM) will geometrically look like precisely 3 moves from now.

examples.title: Basic Trace
examples.explanation: Following variable erasure cleanly.

examples.title: State Trace
examples.explanation: Tracking variable mixing sequentially.

examples.title: Mistake Avoidance
examples.explanation: Tracing forces you to spot simple logical bugs before running the code.

foundation.keyPoint: Roleplay the CPU to build logic thinking.

gameData.q: Which is a valid variable name?
gameData.hint: Letters or underscores only to start.
